core_meaning: <ruby>入<rt>い</rt></ruby>れる means to put something INTO a container, space, or group — the key idea is that something crosses a boundary and ends up inside. It also extends to switching things on, brewing drinks, and accepting someone into an organization.

sentences.jp: <ruby>鍵<rt>かぎ</rt></ruby>、バッグに<ruby>入<rt>い</rt></ruby>れといたよ。
sentences.translation: I put the keys in the bag for you.
sentences.notes: 〜ておく contracted to 〜とく is very natural in casual speech; shows the core meaning of physically placing something inside a container.

sentences.jp: <ruby>砂糖<rt>さとう</rt></ruby>を<ruby>入<rt>い</rt></ruby>れたコーヒーが<ruby>好<rt>す</rt></ruby>きじゃないんだ。
sentences.translation: I don't really like coffee with sugar in it.
sentences.notes: Shows <ruby>入<rt>い</rt></ruby>れる used for adding an ingredient; the past-tense relative clause 〜を<ruby>入<rt>い</rt></ruby>れた modifying a noun is a very common pattern.

sentences.jp: <ruby>部屋<rt>へや</rt></ruby>に<ruby>入<rt>い</rt></ruby>れてもらえますか。
sentences.translation: Could you let me into the room?
sentences.notes: 〜てもらえますか is a polite request; here <ruby>入<rt>い</rt></ruby>れる means to grant access or let someone in, not just placing an object.

sentences.jp: <ruby>新入生<rt>しんにゅうせい</rt></ruby>をサークルに<ruby>入<rt>い</rt></ruby>れるかどうか、<ruby>来週<rt>らいしゅう</rt></ruby><ruby>決<rt>き</rt></ruby>めます。
sentences.translation: We'll decide next week whether to accept the new students into the club.
sentences.notes: Illustrates the extended sense of admitting someone into a group or organization.

sentences.jp: エアコンを<ruby>入<rt>い</rt></ruby>れるのを<ruby>忘<rt>わす</rt></ruby>れてしまい、<ruby>会議室<rt>かいぎしつ</rt></ruby>がとても<ruby>暑<rt>あつ</rt></ruby>くなっておりました。
sentences.translation: I forgot to turn on the air conditioning, and the meeting room became very hot.
sentences.notes: <ruby>入<rt>い</rt></ruby>れる is a common, natural word for switching on appliances like エアコン or <ruby>電気<rt>でんき</rt></ruby>; the humble 〜ておりました marks formal/workplace register.

dont_use: Don't use <ruby>入<rt>い</rt></ruby>れる when something moves into a place under its own power — that's <ruby>入<rt>はい</rt></ruby>る. Also, for turning on electronics like TVs or lights, <ruby>つける</rt></ruby> is often more natural than <ruby>入<rt>い</rt></ruby>れる (though <ruby>入<rt>い</rt></ruby>れる works for appliances like エアコン and some regional/older speech). Don't use <ruby>入<rt>い</rt></ruby>れる to mean 'include' in an abstract or written sense — <ruby>含<rt>ふく</rt></ruby>める or <ruby>盛<rt>も</rt></ruby>り<ruby>込<rt>こ</rt></ruby>む are more appropriate there.

confused_with.contrast: A common mistake is using <ruby>入<rt>い</rt></ruby>れる when the subject moves into somewhere themselves — for example, saying ×<ruby>部屋<rt>へや</rt></ruby>に<ruby>入<rt>い</rt></ruby>れた instead of ○<ruby>部屋<rt>へや</rt></ruby>に<ruby>入<rt>はい</rt></ruby>った (I entered the room). <ruby>入<rt>い</rt></ruby>れる is transitive — someone puts something in — while <ruby>入<rt>はい</rt></ruby>る is intransitive — something or someone goes in on their own. Think of it as the difference between 'put in' (<ruby>入<rt>い</rt></ruby>れる) and 'go in / get in' (<ruby>入<rt>はい</rt></ruby>る).

frequency: <ruby>入<rt>い</rt></ruby>れる is very common in everyday spoken and written Japanese. It appears constantly in daily life — cooking, packing, accepting people into groups, switching on appliances. It is not formal or regional; you will hear it in casual conversation as often as in standard contexts.
anki_hint: <ruby>入<rt>い</rt></ruby>れる is always transitive — you are the one doing the 'putting in'; if something enters by itself, use <ruby>入<rt>はい</rt></ruby>る instead.
